raagul_prakash.name: Raagul Prakash
raagul_prakash.summary:
A Tennessee competitor, Raagul Prakash has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 6 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Raagul competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Raagul has formed effective partnerships:
  • Akshay Karthi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Raagul has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
raagul_prakash.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Raagul Prakash, from Tennessee, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Raagul is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf), and so far, the only thing Raagul has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Raagul saga began at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Raagul has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Raagul has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Raagul showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U17, which means Raagul traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Raagul entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Raagul paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Raagul keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Raagul has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Raagul, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Raagul. We will be here, taking notes.

rachel_chan.name: Rachel Chan
rachel_chan.summary:
Out of New York, Rachel Chan has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 28 entries across 10 tournaments, Rachel is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Rachel has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Rachel especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries).

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Rachel adapts to anyone:
  • Alicia Chang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U13 (5 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Cindy Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Ryan Chan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Rachel has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Rachel isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Rachel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rachel_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rachel Chan, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 10 tournaments, Rachel has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Rachel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rachel saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rachel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Rachel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rachel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alicia Chang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alicia Chang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ryan Chan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rachel entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Rachel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rachel and said 'nah.'

Rachel has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Rachel has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rachel has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rachel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Rachel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Rachel entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Rachel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Rachel keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Rachel has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Rachel, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Rachel. We will be here, taking notes.

rachel_yao.name: Rachel Yao
rachel_yao.summary:
A New Jersey competitor, Rachel Yao is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Rachel in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Rachel is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Rachel is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 GD) • Junior Nationals top-4 (XD U13) • 1 ORC title.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Rachel adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Qiu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Siddhi Thorat (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U13 (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Trisha Mehta (current partner) — 3 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

Rachel is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Rachel is still ascending.

Rachel has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rachel has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Rachel consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Rachel is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
rachel_yao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rachel Yao, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 62 career entries in 23 tournaments, Rachel has 7 titles — a 11% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Rachel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 62 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rachel saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Rachel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Rachel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Rachel experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Rachel has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 MassBad NE OLC, where Rachel entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Rachel does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Rachel has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 7 wins means Rachel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rachel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Rachel can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Rachel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rachel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Qiu: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Qiu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13 — we hope Henry Tan has emotionally recovered.

Of Rachel's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Rachel feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rachel entered 3 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Rachel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rachel and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Rachel 6 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Tournament committees have handed Rachel a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Rachel has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13, where Rachel was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Rachel of is playing it safe. Rachel has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Rachel has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Rachel has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rachel is not just losing locally — Rachel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yao family travels cross-country so Rachel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rachel has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rachel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13, which means Rachel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Rachel entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 25th. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Rachel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 62 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rachel looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rachel's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rachel has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rachel. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Rachel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rachel's texts. The go-to partner is Catherine Chen (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Joseph Yedidiyah Pohan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Rachel's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Rachel has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Then there is the seeding situation. Rachel has been seeded in 10 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Rachel takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U11, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Rachel. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Rachel has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rachel is not just losing locally — Rachel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Rachel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rachel has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rachel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11, which means Rachel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Rachel entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Rachel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 50 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rachel looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rachel's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rachel has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rachel. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

radha_kuchi.name: Radha Kuchi
radha_kuchi.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Radha Kuchi has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 29 entries across 11 tournaments, Radha is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Radha has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Radha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Radha adapts to anyone:
  • Rithika Thatipatri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Krishna Kuchi (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Venkata Shashank Kondaveeti (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Radha has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Radha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
radha_kuchi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Radha Kuchi, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Radha has 29 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Radha saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Radha would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Radha has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Radha has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Radha's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Radha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Radha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Radha's texts. The go-to partner is Rithika Thatipatri (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Venkata Shashank Kondaveeti has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Radha has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Radha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Radha has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Radha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in GS U17, which means Radha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Radha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Radha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Radha. Radha just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Radha has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Radha. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Radha: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

rahul_kashyap_josyula.name: Rahul Kashyap Josyula
rahul_kashyap_josyula.summary:
Out of Texas, Rahul Kashyap Josyula is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 11 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Notably, Rahul has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Rahul especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Rahul has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Rahul's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
rahul_kashyap_josyula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rahul Kashyap Josyula, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Rahul's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Rahul keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Rahul saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rahul plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Rahul has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Shriariman Karthigayan has emotionally recovered.

Rahul has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Rahul has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rahul has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rahul showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Rahul traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Rahul entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Rahul paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Rahul keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Rahul has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Rahul, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Rahul. We will be here, taking notes.

rajat_rawat.name: Rajat Rawat
rajat_rawat.summary:
Out of California, Rajat Rawat has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Rajat's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Rajat has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Rajat especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Rajat has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Atharva Pulijal (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (7 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Atharva Gulla Pulijal — a trusted BD/U17 partner (7 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Alex He — 2 events in BD, best: #4, across 2 seasons
  • Xiaoyue Liu — a trusted XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Rajat has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rajat has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 13 seeded entries across the career, Rajat consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Rajat brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rajat_rawat.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rajat Rawat, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 47 career entries in 21 tournaments, Rajat has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 46 entries where Rajat drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 47 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rajat saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rajat plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 47 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Rajat has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rajat's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Atharva Gulla Pulijal: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Atharva Gulla Pulijal has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Anvita Jamsandekar has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rajat entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Rajat's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rajat and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Rajat has shown up 7 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Rajat has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rajat is not just losing locally — Rajat is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Rawat family travels cross-country so Rajat can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rajat has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rajat showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Rajat traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Rajat entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Rajat paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 47 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Rajat. Rajat just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Rajat has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Rajat. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Rajat: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 13 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ray. Ray just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ray has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ray. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ray: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ray_qiu.name: Ray Qiu
ray_qiu.summary:
Hailing from California, Ray Qiu is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U13 level, with 11 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Ray competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Ray adapts to anyone:

Ray has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ray's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ray_qiu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ray Qiu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ray's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ray keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ray saga began at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ray plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ray has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Benjamin Tsai has emotionally recovered.

Ray has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ray has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ray showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Ray traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Ray entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ray paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ray keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ray has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ray, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ray. We will be here, taking notes.

raymond_haiwen_song.name: Raymond Haiwen Song
raymond_haiwen_song.summary:
Representing Texas, Raymond Haiwen Song has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U17. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Raymond belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Raymond has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Raymond especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U17) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Raymond adapts to anyone:
  • Micah Wei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (16 events, best: #2, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Savannah Kam (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Haziqa H Mathani (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Raymond is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Raymond has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Raymond has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Raymond consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Raymond is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
raymond_haiwen_song.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Raymond Haiwen Song, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 72 career entries in 28 tournaments, Raymond has 6 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Raymond drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 72 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Raymond saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Raymond's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Raymond actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Raymond experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Raymond has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South CRC, where Raymond entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Raymond does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Raymond has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 6 wins means Raymond is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Raymond plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 0 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Raymond can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Raymond has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Raymond's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Micah Wei: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Micah Wei has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Micah Wei has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Raymond's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Raymond entered 9 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Raymond's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Raymond and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Raymond 8 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Raymond has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Raymond is not just losing locally — Raymond is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Song family travels cross-country so Raymond can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Raymond has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Raymond showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Raymond traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Raymond entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Raymond paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 72 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Raymond looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Raymond's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Raymond has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Raymond. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Raymond plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Raymond has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Raymond's texts. The go-to partner is Serena Li (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Nina Kuan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Raymond has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Raymond is not just losing locally — Raymond is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Raymond can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Raymond has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Raymond showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Raymond traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Raymond entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Raymond paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Raymond keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Raymond has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Raymond, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Raymond. We will be here, taking notes.

reanne_chan.name: Reanne Chan
reanne_chan.summary:
A California competitor, Reanne Chan is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Reanne in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Reanne is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U19 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U17) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Reanne has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Emily Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Linden Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in U19 (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Reanne has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Reanne has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
reanne_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Reanne Chan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. And yes, Reanne IS a Junior National Champion — GD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 25-entry career across 9 tournaments with a 4% win rate, which means 24 times — 24! — Reanne went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 25 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Reanne saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Reanne burst onto the scene with a title in GD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Reanne has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Reanne promised and what Reanne has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Reanne actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Reanne is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Reanne would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Reanne has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Reanne has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Reanne's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Reanne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Reanne is 0-for-7. Zero titles in 7 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Reanne and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Reanne needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Reanne has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Reanne's texts. The go-to partner is Emily Liu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19 — we hope Codey Ma has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Reanne entered 6 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Reanne's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Reanne and said 'nah.'

Reanne has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Reanne has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Reanne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U19, which means Reanne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 25 entries, and 24 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Reanne looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Reanne's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Reanne has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Reanne. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ren_watanabe.name: Ren Watanabe
ren_watanabe.summary:
Out of Minnesota, Ren Watanabe is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Ren competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
ren_watanabe.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ren Watanabe, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ren is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Ren has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ren saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ren plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ren has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ren showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Ren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ren entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Ren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ren is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ren wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ren, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

renee_leung.name: Renee Leung
renee_leung.summary:
Out of California, Renee Leung is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Renee in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Renee has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Renee is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 XD) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Renee adapts to anyone:
  • Saee Thorat (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Rishabh Praveen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • William Geng (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Carsten Lam (current partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (2 events)

Renee has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Renee has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Renee consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Renee has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
renee_leung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Renee Leung, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 59 career entries in 23 tournaments, Renee has 5 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 10 entries where Renee drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 59 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Renee saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Renee's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Renee actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Renee is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Renee plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Renee can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Renee has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Renee's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Wu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Wu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Saee Thorat has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Renee's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Renee entered 12 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Renee's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Renee and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Renee has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Renee has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Renee is not just losing locally — Renee is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Leung family travels cross-country so Renee can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Renee has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Renee showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Renee traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Renee entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Renee paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 59 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Renee looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Renee's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Renee has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Renee. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

reshma_rajesh.name: Reshma Rajesh
reshma_rajesh.summary:

A true triple-threat, Reshma competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Reshma has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Reshma's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
reshma_rajesh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Reshma Rajesh, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Reshma's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Reshma keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Reshma saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Reshma plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Reshma has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Abel Daniel Koshy has emotionally recovered.

Reshma has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights.
