And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 Midwest CRC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Reshma keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Reshma has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Reshma, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Reshma. We will be here, taking notes.

reyansh_chalamalasetti.name: Reyansh Chalamalasetti
reyansh_chalamalasetti.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Reyansh Chalamalasetti is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U11) puts Reyansh in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Reyansh has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Reyansh competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U11) • 2 ORC titles.

With 25 different doubles partners over the years, Reyansh adapts to anyone:
  • Qingyao Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Leslie Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Leo Ye (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

Reyansh has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Reyansh has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 15 seeded entries across the career, Reyansh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Reyansh has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
reyansh_chalamalasetti.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Reyansh Chalamalasetti, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Reyansh has 68 career entries across 24 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Reyansh saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Reyansh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Reyansh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Reyansh is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Reyansh experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Reyansh has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Reyansh does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Reyansh has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 3 wins means Reyansh is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Reyansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Reyansh is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Reyansh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Reyansh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Reyansh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Reyansh's texts. The go-to partner is Vignan Chandrasekaran (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Aarav Reddy Mukkamala has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Reyansh has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Reyansh a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Reyansh has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11, where Reyansh was seeded #1 and finished 33rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Reyansh has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Reyansh is not just losing locally — Reyansh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chalamalasetti family travels cross-country so Reyansh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Reyansh has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Reyansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Reyansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Reyansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Reyansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 68 entries, and 65 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Reyansh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Reyansh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Reyansh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Reyansh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Reyansh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
reyansh_sehgal.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Reyansh Sehgal, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Reyansh is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Reyansh has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Reyansh saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Reyansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Reyansh has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Reyansh has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Reyansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Reyansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Reyansh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Reyansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Reyansh keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Reyansh has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Reyansh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Reyansh. We will be here, taking notes.

rheya_huang.name: Rheya Huang
rheya_huang.summary:
Representing Minnesota, Rheya Huang is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 16 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Rheya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Rheya adapts to anyone:
  • Evelyn Xiaoran Ge (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Rheya has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Rheya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rheya_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rheya Huang, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 16 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 16-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Rheya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Rheya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Rheya saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rheya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Rheya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rheya's texts. The go-to partner is Evelyn Xiaoran Ge (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Derek Royce Chan has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rheya has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rheya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Rheya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Rheya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Rheya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 16 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Rheya. Rheya just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Rheya has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Rheya. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Rheya: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

riana_soprala.name: Riana Soprala
riana_soprala.summary:
Representing Texas, Riana Soprala is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GD U11, XD U11) puts Riana in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Riana has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Riana especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U11, XD U11) • 1 ORC title.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Riana adapts to anyone:
  • Magadheesan Velu (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Adalyn Puah (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Xiner Hu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Rianshi Kuchi (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Riana is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Riana has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Riana has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Riana consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Riana brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
riana_soprala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Riana Soprala, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Riana has 47 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Riana saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South CRC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Riana's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Riana experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Riana has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, where Riana entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Riana does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Riana has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 4 wins means Riana is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Riana plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Riana is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Riana and suddenly the wins start appearing — 4 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Riana needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Riana has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Riana's texts. The go-to partner is Magadheesan Velu (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Darmikh Pallabothula has emotionally recovered.

Of Riana's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Riana feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Riana has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Riana a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Riana has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in GS U11, where Riana was seeded #1 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Riana has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Riana is not just losing locally — Riana is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Soprala family travels cross-country so Riana can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Riana has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Riana showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Riana traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Riana entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Riana paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 47 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Riana looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Riana's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Riana has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Riana. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

rianshi_kuchi.name: Rianshi Kuchi
rianshi_kuchi.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Rianshi Kuchi has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 2 titles across 10 tournaments and 29 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Rianshi keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Rianshi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Rianshi adapts to anyone:
  • Siddharth Kuchi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (8 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Riana Soprala (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)
  • Aditi Malladi (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Rianshi is still ascending.

Rianshi has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Rianshi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
rianshi_kuchi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rianshi Kuchi, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Rianshi has 29 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Rianshi saga began at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Capital NE OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Rianshi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Rianshi would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Rianshi has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Rianshi has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Rianshi's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rianshi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Rianshi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Rianshi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rianshi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Siddharth Kuchi: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Siddharth Kuchi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Siddharth Kuchi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Rianshi's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Rianshi has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rianshi has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rianshi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Rianshi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Rianshi entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 49th. That is an average placement of 31st, which is a polite way of saying Rianshi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 29 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rianshi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rianshi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rianshi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rianshi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

richard_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Richard Liu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Richard has 13 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Richard saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Richard plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Richard has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U15 — we hope Charlie Chen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Richard has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Richard has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Richard has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Richard showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Richard traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Richard entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 65th. That is an average placement of 57th, which is a polite way of saying Richard paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Richard keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Richard has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Richard, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Richard. We will be here, taking notes.

The Ricky saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ricky plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Ricky has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ricky's texts. The go-to partner is Tyron Mai (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Tyron Mai has emotionally recovered.

Ricky has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. So here we are: 5 seasons, 27 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Ricky just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ricky has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ricky. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ricky: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Ridha is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ridha wants to be. ridha_mansoor.name: Ridha Mansoor
ridha_mansoor.summary:
Representing Illinois, Ridha Mansoor has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 16 entries across 7 tournaments, Ridha is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Ridha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Ridha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Ananthaa Nagarajan (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ridha has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Ridha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ridha_mansoor.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ridha Mansoor, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 16 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 16-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ridha's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ridha keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ridha saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ridha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ridha has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Ananthaa Nagarajan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U13 — we hope Kaitlyn Kim has emotionally recovered.

Ridha has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ridha has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ridha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Ridha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Ridha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Ridha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 16 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ridha. Ridha just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ridha has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ridha. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ridha: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ridhiman_krishna_patwari.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ridhiman Krishna Patwari, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ridhiman has 20 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ridhiman saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ridhiman plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ridhiman has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Mishael Shaik (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Mishael Shaik has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Ridhiman has shown up 6 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ridhiman is not just losing locally — Ridhiman is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Patwari family travels cross-country so Ridhiman can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ridhiman has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ridhiman showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Ridhiman traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Ridhiman entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Ridhiman paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ridhiman keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ridhiman has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ridhiman, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ridhiman. We will be here, taking notes.

rishabh_praveen.name: Rishabh Praveen
rishabh_praveen.summary:
Representing California, Rishabh Praveen stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

A true triple-threat, Rishabh competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Rishabh has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Pranav Vivek (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Elena Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Cayden Wang — a trusted BD/U15 partner (9 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Celine Ao — a trusted U15/XD partner (4 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Rishabh has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rishabh has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Rishabh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Rishabh is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
rishabh_praveen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rishabh Praveen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 68 career entries in 24 tournaments, Rishabh has 1 title — a 1% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 67 entries where Rishabh drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 68 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rishabh saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Rishabh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Rishabh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Rishabh is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Rishabh experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Rishabh has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Rishabh entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Rishabh does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Rishabh has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Rishabh is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rishabh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Rishabh is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Rishabh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rishabh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Cayden Wang: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Cayden Wang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Kenneth Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rishabh entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Rishabh's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rishabh and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Rishabh has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rishabh is not just losing locally — Rishabh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Praveen family travels cross-country so Rishabh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rishabh has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rishabh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Rishabh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Rishabh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Rishabh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 68 entries, and 67 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rishabh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rishabh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rishabh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rishabh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

rishika_nanda.name: Rishika Nanda
rishika_nanda.summary:
Rishika Nanda has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 5 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Rishika competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

In doubles, Rishika has formed effective partnerships:
  • Prithvi Roopa Pradeep (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Rishika has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Rishika brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rishika_nanda.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rishika Nanda, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Rishika is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Rishika has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Rishika saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rishika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Rishika has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rishika has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rishika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U15, which means Rishika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Rishika entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Rishika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Rishika keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Rishika has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Rishika, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Rishika. We will be here, taking notes.

rishita_tubati.name: Rishita Tubati
rishita_tubati.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Rishita Tubati has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Rishita's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Rishita has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Rishita competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Rishita has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Shagunpreet Kaur (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Huaiyuan Ni — 2 events in XD, best: #4
  • Krisha Tyagi — a trusted GD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Rishita has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Rishita has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Rishita brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rishita_tubati.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rishita Tubati, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Rishita has 30 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Rishita saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, where Rishita burst onto the scene with a title in GS U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Rishita has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Rishita promised and what Rishita has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Rishita actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Rishita is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Rishita would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Rishita has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Rishita has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Rishita's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rishita plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Rishita can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Rishita has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rishita's texts. The go-to partner is Krisha Tyagi (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Nithesh Haribabu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Rishita's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Rishita has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Rishita has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rishita is not just losing locally — Rishita is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tubati family travels cross-country so Rishita can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rishita has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rishita showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means Rishita traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Rishita entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Rishita paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 30 entries, and 28 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rishita looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rishita's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rishita has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rishita. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ritchie_timothy.name: Ritchie Timothy
ritchie_timothy.summary:
Out of California, Ritchie Timothy is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 3 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Ritchie competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Ritchie has competed at both U13 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
ritchie_timothy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ritchie Timothy, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ritchie is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, and so far, the only thing Ritchie has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ritchie saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ritchie has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ritchie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in BS U13, which means Ritchie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Season after season, Ritchie keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ritchie has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ritchie, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ritchie. We will be here, taking notes.

ritchie_k_timothy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ritchie k Timothy, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins.
