Ritchie is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ritchie wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ritchie, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

rithik_rasamsetti.name: Rithik Rasamsetti
rithik_rasamsetti.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Rithik Rasamsetti has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 BS, U17 BD, U17 XD, U13 XD, U13 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 31 career titles across 32 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Rithik is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Rithik competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 8 singles titles, 6 doubles titles, 9 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BS, U17 BD, U17 XD, U13 XD, U13 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U15, XD U11, BS U13, BS U11, XD U13, BD U13, XD U15) • 14 ORC titles.

Rithik has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Noah Chien (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD/U17, across 2 seasons)
  • Joanne Li — 7 events in U17/XD, producing 4 titles together, spanning 3 seasons

Rithik has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rithik has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Rithik has been seeded #1 or #2 in 15 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 31 titles, Rithik has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
rithik_rasamsetti.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rithik Rasamsetti, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Rithik is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Rithik has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Rithik would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Rithik, we kept reading — all 90 entries across 32 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 34% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Rithik held up a trophy, there were roughly 1 other times Rithik walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Rithik saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Rithik burst onto the scene with a title in BS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Rithik has delivered on some of those promises: 31 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Rithik promised and what Rithik has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 14 titles in 28 entries — a stretch where Rithik actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 6 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Rithik experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Rithik has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13), where Rithik entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Rithik does own 31 actual titles, so it is not like Rithik has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 31 wins means Rithik is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rithik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 8 singles, 6 doubles, 9 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Rithik can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Rithik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rithik's texts. The most successful partnership is with Seth Li — 14 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Seth Li deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Seth Li a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Shriariman Karthigayan has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Rithik has been seeded in 19 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Rithik takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 South Houston CRC in BS U15, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Rithik. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Rithik of is playing it safe. Rithik has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Rithik has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 28 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Rithik has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rithik is not just losing locally — Rithik is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 34% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Rasamsetti family travels cross-country so Rithik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rithik has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rithik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, which means Rithik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Rithik entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Rithik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 31 titles to the name, 90 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rithik looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rithik's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rithik has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rithik. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

rithvika_santhakumar.name: Rithvika Santhakumar
rithvika_santhakumar.summary:
Out of Virginia, Rithvika Santhakumar has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 26 entries in 10 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Rithvika has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Rithvika especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Rithvika adapts to anyone:
  • Wayne Chien (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Rithvika has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Rithvika isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Rithvika brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rithvika_santhakumar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rithvika Santhakumar, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 26 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 26-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Rithvika's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Rithvika keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Rithvika saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rithvika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 26 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Rithvika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rithvika's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15 — we hope Siddhanth Selvanathan has emotionally recovered.

Rithvika has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Rithvika has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rithvika has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rithvika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Rithvika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Rithvika entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Rithvika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 26 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Rithvika. Rithvika just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Rithvika has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Rithvika. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Rithvika: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

riya_suresh.name: Riya Suresh
riya_suresh.summary:
Out of Texas, Riya Suresh is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Riya has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Riya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 24 different doubles partners over the years, Riya adapts to anyone:
  • Yuxuan Zeng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Napat Srisalaipaisal — a trusted U17/U19/XD partner (8 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Lydia Ng — 2 events in GD, best: #4, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Riya has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Riya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Riya is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
riya_suresh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Riya Suresh, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 69 career entries in 27 tournaments, Riya has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 33 entries where Riya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Riya saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in GD U17 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Riya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Riya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Riya is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Riya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Riya has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Riya does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Riya has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Riya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Riya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Riya is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Riya and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Riya needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Riya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Riya's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Napat Srisalaipaisal: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Napat Srisalaipaisal has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Napat Srisalaipaisal has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Riya entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Riya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Riya and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Riya has shown up 11 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Riya has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Riya is not just losing locally — Riya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Suresh family travels cross-country so Riya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Riya has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Riya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Riya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Riya entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Riya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 67 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Riya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Riya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Riya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Riya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

robert_shekhtman.name: Robert Shekhtman
robert_shekhtman.summary:
A Massachusetts competitor, Robert Shekhtman wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in BS U17. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 2 career victories across 3 tournaments, Robert has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Robert has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Robert especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (BS U17) • 1 ORC title.

In doubles, Robert has formed effective partnerships:
  • Joline Siu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Kai Chong (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

Robert has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
robert_shekhtman.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Robert Shekhtman, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BS U17. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 8 career entries across 3 tournaments, 2 total wins, and 6 losses. A 25% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Robert has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Robert saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Robert burst onto the scene with a title in BS U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Robert has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Robert promised and what Robert has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Robert plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Robert can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

Robert has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Robert has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Robert has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Robert showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U19, which means Robert traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 8 entries, and 6 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Robert looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Robert's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Robert has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Robert. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

rocky_khuu.name: Rocky Khuu
rocky_khuu.summary:
A California competitor, Rocky Khuu is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 13 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Rocky especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Rocky has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Shravan Chockalingam (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Grace Zichen Qu Zhao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

Rocky has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rocky has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Rocky brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rocky_khuu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rocky Khuu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Rocky has 13 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Rocky saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rocky plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Rocky has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rocky is not just losing locally — Rocky is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Khuu family travels cross-country so Rocky can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rocky has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rocky showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Rocky traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Rocky entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Rocky paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Rocky keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Rocky has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Rocky, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Rocky. We will be here, taking notes.

rocky_yang.name: Rocky Yang
rocky_yang.summary:

Notably, Rocky has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Rocky is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 XD, U15 BS) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U17, XD U19) • 3 ORC titles.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Rocky adapts to anyone:
  • Sydney Li (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Zicheng Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Rocky is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Rocky has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rocky has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Rocky consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Rocky has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
rocky_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rocky Yang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 69 career entries in 24 tournaments, Rocky has 4 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Rocky drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rocky saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Rocky's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Rocky actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Rocky is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Rocky experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Rocky has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Rocky does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Rocky has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Rocky is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rocky plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Rocky is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Rocky and suddenly the wins start appearing — 4 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Rocky needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Rocky has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Rocky's texts. The go-to partner is Nicole Krawczyk (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rocky entered 12 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Rocky's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rocky and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Rocky has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Rocky has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rocky is not just losing locally — Rocky is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Rocky can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rocky has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rocky showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U19, which means Rocky traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Rocky entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Rocky paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 65 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rocky looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rocky's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rocky has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rocky. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Rohan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
rohan_boreddy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rohan Boreddy, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Rohan is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf), and so far, the only thing Rohan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Rohan saga began at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Rohan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Rohan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Rohan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

rohan_chandrakan_kokje.name: Rohan Chandrakan Kokje

Rohan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Rohan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
rohan_chandrakan_kokje.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rohan Chandrakan Kokje, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Rohan is right on schedule. The Rohan saga began at 2024 SBC NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Rohan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

Rohan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Rohan wants to be. rohan_kumar_jha.name: Rohan Kumar Jha
rohan_kumar_jha.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Rohan Kumar Jha is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 9 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Rohan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NorCal, South, Rohan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
rohan_kumar_jha.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rohan Kumar Jha, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Rohan is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 South Frisco ORC, and so far, the only thing Rohan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Rohan saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rohan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Rohan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Yves Judson Homayoon has emotionally recovered.

Rohan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rohan has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rohan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Rohan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Rohan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Rohan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Rohan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Rohan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Rohan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

rohan_rai.name: Rohan Rai
rohan_rai.summary:
Representing California, Rohan Rai is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U19 level, with 7 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Rohan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

In doubles, Rohan has formed effective partnerships:
  • Anish Bansal (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Rohan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
rohan_rai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rohan Rai, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Rohan has 7 career entries across 3 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Rohan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rohan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Rohan has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Rohan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rohan has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rohan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Rohan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Rohan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Rohan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Rohan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Rohan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Rohan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Rohan. We will be here, taking notes.

What makes Ronith especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Ronith adapts to anyone:
  • Hannah George (current primary partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 19 events in Mixed/U17/XD, spanning 5 seasons
  • Bettie Huang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (3 events, best: #5)
  • Srinivas Raghav Subash (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2024-2025 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Ronith is still ascending.

Ronith has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ronith has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Ronith consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Ronith is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ronith_a_manikonda.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ronith A Manikonda, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 63 career entries in 26 tournaments, Ronith has 6 titles — a 10% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 9 entries where Ronith drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 63 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ronith saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ronith's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Ronith actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 13 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ronith plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ronith can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Ronith has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ronith's texts. The go-to partner is Hannah George (19 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Nishchith G Manjunatha has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ronith's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ronith entered 8 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ronith's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ronith and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ronith has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Ronith has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ronith is not just losing locally — Ronith is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Manikonda family travels cross-country so Ronith can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ronith has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ronith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Ronith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Ronith entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Ronith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 63 entries, and 57 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ronith looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ronith's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ronith has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ronith. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Rudransh would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Rudransh has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Rudransh has been sent home before the championship match even started. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Rudransh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

ruohan_joy_du.name: Ruohan Joy Du
ruohan_joy_du.summary:
Out of Virginia, Ruohan Joy Du has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Ruohan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Ruohan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Ruohan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 32 different doubles partners over the years, Ruohan adapts to anyone:
  • Jonathan Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Ruohan has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ruohan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Ruohan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ruohan_joy_du.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ruohan Joy Du, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 77 career entries in 27 tournaments, Ruohan has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Ruohan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 77 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ruohan saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ruohan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 27 entries — a stretch where Ruohan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ruohan is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Ruohan would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 11 times Ruohan has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 11 times Ruohan has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Ruohan's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ruohan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ruohan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 32 of them, to be precise. Ruohan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ruohan's texts. The go-to partner is Nila Ramesh (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ethan Yip Ly has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ruohan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ruohan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ruohan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ruohan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ruohan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Ruohan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ruohan is not just losing locally — Ruohan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Du family travels cross-country so Ruohan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ruohan has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ruohan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Ruohan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Ruohan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Ruohan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 77 entries, and 75 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ruohan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ruohan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ruohan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ruohan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ruoxi_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ruoxi Wang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ruoxi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ruoxi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ruoxi saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ruoxi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ruoxi has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Allen Wu has emotionally recovered.

Ruoxi has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ruoxi has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ruoxi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Ruoxi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Ruoxi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Ruoxi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ruoxi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ruoxi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ruoxi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ruoxi. We will be here, taking notes.

ryan_chan.name: Ryan Chan
ryan_chan.summary:
A New York competitor, Ryan Chan is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 14 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Ryan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Ryan adapts to anyone:
  • Rachel Chan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Ryan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ryan_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan Chan, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ryan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ryan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ryan saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ryan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ryan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Edward Chang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Nadia Lin has emotionally recovered.

Ryan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ryan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ryan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ryan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ryan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ryan. We will be here, taking notes.
