ryan_chu.name: Ryan Chu
ryan_chu.summary:
A Washington competitor, Ryan Chu is a serial champion on the local circuit, currently competing at the U15 level, racking up 6 titles across 17 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Ryan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Ryan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Ryan adapts to anyone:
  • Aiden Ziran Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Grace Eunhye Park (current partner) — 4 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Stephen Huang (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ryan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Ryan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Ryan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 6 titles, Ryan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
ryan_chu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan Chu, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ryan has 44 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 14% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ryan saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ryan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Ryan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ryan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ryan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Ryan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ryan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aiden Ziran Xu: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aiden Ziran Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Jocelyn Chiang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ryan's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ryan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Then there is the seeding situation. Ryan has been seeded in 9 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Ryan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #4 at 2024 OBA NW OLC in BS U13, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Ryan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Ryan of is playing it safe. Ryan has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Ryan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Ryan has built a permanent residence on that line. Ryan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 44 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ryan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ryan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ryan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ryan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ryan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
ryan_kavumpurath.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan KAVUMPURATH, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ryan is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 ABC NorCal OLC, and so far, the only thing Ryan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ryan saga began at 2025 ABC NorCal OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 ABC NorCal OLC in BS U15, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 ABC NorCal OLC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 ABC NorCal OLC, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ryan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ryan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ryan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

ryan_ma.name: Ryan Ma
ryan_ma.summary:
A California competitor, Ryan Ma has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U19 BS, U19 BD, U19 XD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 15 career titles across 9 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Ryan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 5 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 BS, U19 BD, U19 XD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U19) • 8 ORC titles.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Ryan adapts to anyone:
  • Veronica Yang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in U19/XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kyle Wang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Daniel Zhou — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 15 titles, Ryan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ryan_ma.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan Ma, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Ryan is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Ryan has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Ryan would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Ryan, we kept reading — all 25 entries across 9 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 60% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Ryan held up a trophy, there were roughly 0 other times Ryan walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Ryan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ryan burst onto the scene with a title in BS U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Ryan has delivered on some of those promises: 15 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Ryan promised and what Ryan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 7 titles in 9 entries — a stretch where Ryan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ryan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ryan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Ryan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The most successful partnership is with Veronica Yang — 7 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Veronica Yang deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Veronica Yang a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting.

Ryan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U19 XD, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 15 titles to the name, 25 entries, and 10 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ryan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ryan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ryan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ryan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 13th to 17th. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ryan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ryan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ryan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

ryan_shih.name: Ryan Shih
ryan_shih.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Ryan Shih knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 44 entries across 18 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Ryan has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Ryan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Ryan adapts to anyone:
  • Gagan Polineni (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Naresh Sriram (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U17, Ryan is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Ryan brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ryan has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Ryan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Ryan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ryan_shih.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan Shih, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ryan has 44 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ryan saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ryan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 19 entries — a stretch where Ryan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ryan is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ryan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ryan is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ryan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ryan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Ryan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ryan's texts. The go-to partner is Gagan Polineni (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Gabby XZ Xiang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ryan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ryan has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Ryan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ryan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 44 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ryan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ryan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ryan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ryan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ryan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Ryan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Ryan adapts to anyone:
  • Pranav Rao — a trusted BD partner (6 events, best: #4)

Ryan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Ryan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Ryan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ryan_tiong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ryan Tiong, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ryan has 21 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ryan saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ryan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ryan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Pranav Rao: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Pranav Rao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope George Shen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Ryan has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Ryan has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ryan is not just losing locally — Ryan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tiong family travels cross-country so Ryan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ryan has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ryan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ryan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ryan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ryan. We will be here, taking notes.

Ryan has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ryan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Ryan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Ryan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ryan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ryan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ryan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ryan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ryan. We will be here, taking notes.

rylan_tan.name: Rylan Tan
rylan_tan.summary:
Hailing from California, Rylan Tan is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Rylan in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

A true triple-threat, Rylan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 BD, U19 BS, U15 BS, U19 XD) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U13, BD U13) • 2 ORC titles.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Rylan adapts to anyone:
  • Micah Cruz (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in U15/U19/XD, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Rylan has competed across U13, U15 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Rylan has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Rylan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Rylan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
rylan_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Rylan Tan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Rylan IS a Junior National Champion — XD U13, BD U13 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 19-entry career across 8 tournaments with a 21% win rate, which means 15 times — 15! — Rylan went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 19 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Rylan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Rylan burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Rylan has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Rylan promised and what Rylan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 2 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Rylan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Rylan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Rylan has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Rylan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (U15 BS, U15 BD). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Rylan does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Rylan has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 4 wins means Rylan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Rylan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Rylan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Rylan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Micah Cruz (4 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U15 — we hope Justin Xia has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Rylan entered 5 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Rylan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Rylan and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Rylan is not just losing locally — Rylan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 21% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tan family travels cross-country so Rylan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Rylan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Rylan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U15, which means Rylan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 19 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Rylan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Rylan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Rylan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Rylan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
